title: कृष्णकर्णामृतम् (प्रमुख श्लोक)
devanagariText:
॥ श्री कृष्णकर्णामृतम् ॥

॥ श्री बिल्वमङ्गल कृतम् ॥

चिन्तामणिर्जयति सोमगिरिर्गुरुर्मे
शिक्षागुरुश्च भगवान् शिखिपिञ्छमौलिः।
यत्पादकल्पतरुपल्लवशेखरेषु
लीलाफलं च करुणा च निधीयते ॥ १॥

कस्तूरीतिलकं ललाटफलके वक्षःस्थले कौस्तुभं
नासाग्रे नवमौक्तिकं करतले वेणुं करे कङ्कणम्।
सर्वाङ्गे हरिचन्दनं च कलयन् कण्ठे च मुक्तावलिं
गोपस्त्रीपरिवेष्टितो विजयते गोपालचूडामणिः॥ २॥

बर्हापीडं नटवरवपुः कर्णयोः कर्णिकारं
बिभ्रद्वासः कनककपिशं वैजयन्तीं च मालाम्।
रन्ध्रान्वेणोरधरसुधया पूरयन्गोपवृन्दैः
वृन्दारण्यं स्वपदरमणं प्राविशद्गीतकीर्तिः॥ ३॥

मधुरं मधुरं वपुरस्य विभोः
मधुरं मधुरं वदनं मधुरम्।
मधुरं मधुरं हसितं मधुरम्
मधुरं मधुरं हृदयं मधुरम्॥ ४॥

मधुरं मधुरं नयनं मधुरं
मधुरं मधुरं तिलकं मधुरम्।
मधुरं मधुरं वसनं मधुरम्
मधुरं मधुरं गमनं मधुरम्॥ ५॥

मधुरं मधुरं वेणुर्मधुरं
मधुरं मधुरं पदं मधुरम्।
मधुरं मधुरं नृत्यं मधुरम्
मधुरं मधुरं स्मितं मधुरम्॥ ६॥

श्रीकृष्णं कमलापतिं वसुदेवं
देवकी नन्दनम्।
नन्दगोपकुमारं वन्दे
जगद्गुरुं गोपिकाप्रियम्॥ ७॥

सौन्दर्यसारसर्वस्वं
गोपालमखिलार्तिहम्।
आनन्दचिन्मयं कृष्णं
वन्दे जगदीश्वरम्॥ ८॥

॥ इति श्री बिल्वमङ्गल विरचितम् कृष्णकर्णामृतम् प्रमुख श्लोकाः सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Krishna Karnamrutam ||

|| Shri Bilvamangala Kritam ||

Chintamanir Jayati Somagirih Gurur Me
Shiksha Gurushcha Bhagavan Shikhipinchhamaulih |
Yat Pada Kalpataru Pallava Shekhareshu
Lila Phalam Cha Karuna Cha Nidhiyate || 1 ||

Kasturi Tilakam Lalata Phalake Vakshah Sthale Kaustubham
Nasagre Nava Mauktikam Karatale Venum Kare Kankanam |
Sarvange Harichandanam Cha Kalayan Kanthe Cha Muktavalim
GopastrI Pariveshtito Vijayate Gopala Chudamanih || 2 ||

Barhapidam Natavaravapuh Karnayoh Karnikaram
Bibhrad Vasah Kanaka Kapisham Vaijayantim Cha Malam |
Randhran Venor Adhara Sudhaya Purayan Gopavrindaih
Vrindāranyam Svapadaramanam Pravishad Gitakirtih || 3 ||

Madhuram Madhuram Vapur Asya Vibhoh
Madhuram Madhuram Vadanam Madhuram |
Madhuram Madhuram Hasitam Madhuram
Madhuram Madhuram Hridayam Madhuram || 4 ||

Madhuram Madhuram Nayanam Madhuram
Madhuram Madhuram Tilakam Madhuram |
Madhuram Madhuram Vasanam Madhuram
Madhuram Madhuram Gamanam Madhuram || 5 ||

Madhuram Madhuram Venur Madhuram
Madhuram Madhuram Padam Madhuram |
Madhuram Madhuram Nrityam Madhuram
Madhuram Madhuram Smitam Madhuram || 6 ||

Shri Krishnam Kamalapatim Vasudevam
Devaki Nandanam |
Nanda Gopa Kumaram Vande
Jagadgurum Gopika Priyam || 7 ||

Saundarya Sara Sarvasvam
Gopalam Akhilaartiham |
Ananda Chinmayam Krishnam
Vande Jagadishvaram || 8 ||

|| Iti Shri Bilvamangala Virachitam Krishna Karnamrutam Pramukh Shlokah Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
चिन्तामणि (चिन्तामणि नामक गुरु) जयवन्त हों, सोमगिरि मेरे गुरु हैं, और मयूरपिच्छ मुकुटधारी भगवान कृष्ण मेरे शिक्षा गुरु हैं, जिनके चरणरूपी कल्पवृक्ष के पल्लवों में लीला और करुणा दोनों निहित हैं।

ललाट पर कस्तूरी तिलक, वक्षस्थल पर कौस्तुभ मणि, नासाग्र पर नवीन मोती, हाथ में वंशी, कलाई में कंकण, सर्वांग पर चन्दन, कण्ठ में मुक्तामाला — गोपियों से परिवेष्टित गोपालचूड़ामणि विजयी हों।

मयूरपिच्छ मुकुट धारण किए, नटवर रूप, कानों में कर्णिकार पुष्प, स्वर्णिम वस्त्र, वैजयन्ती माला पहने, वंशी के छिद्रों को अधर-सुधा से पूरते हुए, गोपों के साथ गीत-कीर्ति वाले कृष्ण ने वृन्दावन में प्रवेश किया।

मधुराष्टकम्: इस विभु का शरीर मधुर है, मुख मधुर है, हँसी मधुर है, हृदय मधुर है, नेत्र मधुर हैं, तिलक मधुर है, वस्त्र मधुर हैं, चाल मधुर है, वेणु मधुर है, चरण मधुर हैं, नृत्य मधुर है, मुस्कान मधुर है — सब कुछ मधुर ही मधुर है।
benefits: Composed by Bilvamangala Thakur, one of the greatest Krishna devotees
Contains the famous Madhurashtakam describing Krishna's all-pervading sweetness
Deepens love and devotion for Lord Krishna
Includes iconic verse describing Krishna's ornaments and beauty
Fills the heart with divine bliss and rasa
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Krishna, Krishna Karnamrutam Key Verses (कृष्णकर्णामृतम् (प्रमुख श्लोक)) stands as a sacred prayer. Attributed to Krishna Karnamrutam (Bilvamangala Thakur), this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Regular recitation is believed to contain the famous Madhurashtakam describing Krishna's all-pervading sweetness, composed by Bilvamangala Thakur, one of the greatest Krishna devotees, and include iconic verse describing Krishna's ornaments and beauty. Recitation is particularly auspicious on Wednesday (Budhvar) and Friday (Shukravar) and during Janmashtami, Holi, and Radha Ashtami. Comprising 8 verses, it can be completed in approximately 5 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Krishna Karnamrutam Key Verses in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Krishna Karnamrutam key verses by Bilvamangala with Madhurashtakam in Sanskrit and Hindi meaning. Beautiful Krishna bhakti poetry.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Krishna Karnamrita?
seoFaqs.answer: The Krishna Karnamrita (कृष्णकर्णामृतम्, literally Nectar for the Ears about Krishna) is a celebrated Sanskrit devotional poem in praise of Lord Krishna, composed by Bilvamangala Thakur (also known by his pen-name Lilashuka, the parrot of leela), the South Indian Vaishnava saint-poet traditionally placed in the 12th-14th century CE. The work consists of three centuries (shatakas) of verses — about 300 shlokas — meditating on every detail of Krishna's beauty, ornaments, leelas in Vrindavan, the gopis' love, Yashoda's vatsalya and the supreme rasa of Krishna-bhakti. Composed in elaborate, musical Sanskrit metres (especially shardulavikridita and sragdhara), the Karnamrita is considered one of the supreme Krishna-kavyas of Sanskrit literature alongside Jayadeva's Gita Govinda. Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu treasured the Karnamrita above all other texts and brought a copy back from his South Indian tour. (Source: Krishna Karnamrita; Bilvamangala Thakur tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Who was Bilvamangala Thakur (Lilashuka)?
seoFaqs.answer: Bilvamangala Thakur (also called Lilashuka, the Parrot of Krishna's Leela) was a South Indian Sanskrit poet and supreme Krishna-bhakta traditionally placed between the 12th and 14th century CE. The most famous biographical legend: as a young Brahmin, Bilvamangala was infatuated with a courtesan named Chintamani; one stormy night he crossed a river clinging to a corpse mistaking it for a log and climbed her wall by a serpent he mistook for a rope. When she shamed him saying that if he had directed even a fraction of this passion towards Krishna he would have attained him, he immediately renounced the world. He is said to have later blinded himself when his eyes again strayed towards a woman — and yet Krishna himself appeared to him in Vrindavan and led the blind poet by the hand. The opening verse of the Karnamrita — Chintamanir jayati Somagirir Gurur Me — honours Chintamani (his catalyst for renunciation) and Somagiri (his guru). (Source: Bilvamangala biographical tradition; Karnamrita verse 1.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the famous opening verse Chintamanir Jayati Somagirih?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 1 of the Krishna Karnamrita is a celebrated invocation: Chintamanir jayati Somagirir Gurur Me, Shiksha-gurushcha Bhagavan Shikhi-pinchha-maulih, yat-pada-kalpataru-pallava-shekhareshu, lila-phalam cha karuna cha nidhiyate — Victory to Chintamani (his initial guru), to Somagiri my guru, and to the Lord himself the peacock-feather-crowned Krishna who is my shiksha-guru (instructional guru), in whose lotus feet's wish-fulfilling-tree-shoots are deposited (nidhiyate) both the fruit of leela and the fruit of compassion. The verse is famous because it acknowledges three gurus: (1) Chintamani — by tradition the courtesan whose biting words turned Bilvamangala towards God, here memorialised as the catalyst-guru; (2) Somagiri — his diksha-guru who initiated him into Krishna-bhakti; (3) Krishna himself (Shikhi-pinchha-maulih, the One with peacock-plume crown) as the inner shiksha-guru. The verse is one of the most quoted opening invocations in Vaishnava literature. (Source: Krishna Karnamrita verse 1.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the famous Kasturi-tilakam verse describing Krishna's ornaments?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 2 — Kasturi-tilakam lalata-phalake vakshah-sthale Kaustubham, nasagre nava-mauktikam karatale venum kare kankanam, sarvange harichandanam cha kalayan kanthe cha muktavalim, gopastri pariveshtito vijayate Gopala-chudamanih — is one of the most beloved descriptive verses in all Krishna-kavya. It describes Krishna ornament-by-ornament: a kasturi (musk) tilak on the forehead-board (lalata-phalake), the Kaustubha jewel on the chest, a fresh pearl (nava-mauktika) at the tip of the nose, the venu (flute) in his palm, kankana (bracelet) on his wrist, harichandana (sandal) paste on every limb, a string of pearls (muktavali) on his throat — surrounded by the gopis (gopastri-pariveshtita), the crest-jewel of all cowherds (gopala-chudamanih) shines in victory. The verse is a complete dhyana of Krishna in his Vrindavan-rasika form and is recited as the principal Krishna-dhyana-shloka in many Vaishnava sampradayas. (Source: Krishna Karnamrita verse 2.)

seoFaqs.question: How is the Krishna Karnamrita related to the Madhurashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 4-6 of the Krishna Karnamrita (Madhuram Madhuram Vapur Asya Vibhoh, etc.) are an extended meditation on the all-pervading madhurya (sweetness) of Krishna — every limb, ornament, action and association of Krishna is madhuram (sweet, sweet, sweet). This passage is one of the principal sources from which the famous Madhurashtakam of Vallabhacharya draws its inspiration: Vallabhacharya's eight verses (Adharam madhuram, vadanam madhuram...) systematise and crystallise the rasa already abundantly present in Bilvamangala's Karnamrita. Both texts establish madhurya-rasa as the supreme bhava in Krishna-bhakti. The Karnamrita pre-dates Vallabhacharya's Pushtimarga and is considered one of the major scriptural fountainheads of madhurya-bhakti, drawn upon by all four major Krishna-sampradayas (Pushtimarga, Gaudiya, Nimbarka, Madhva-Gaudiya). (Sources: Krishna Karnamrita verses 4-6; Vallabhacharya Madhurashtakam.)

seoFaqs.question: Why did Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu treasure the Krishna Karnamrita?
seoFaqs.answer: According to the Chaitanya Charitamrita (the principal hagiography of Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu by Krishnadasa Kaviraja), when Chaitanya Mahaprabhu (1486-1534 CE) made his great South Indian pilgrimage in c. 1510-1512 CE, he found and treasured three texts in particular: the Brahma Samhita (the Brahma-stuti chapter found at Adi-Keshava temple in Travancore), the Krishna Karnamrita of Bilvamangala, and the Mukunda-Mala-Stotra of King Kulashekhara. He brought handwritten copies of these back to Bengal and personally distributed them to his close associates (especially to Raghunatha Dasa Goswami and the other Goswamis). The Krishna Karnamrita became the foundational rasa-shastra of the Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition. Rupa Goswami's Bhakti-Rasamrita-Sindhu draws extensively on the Karnamrita, and the famous Pushpanjali — kesha-pasha-vinyasa, Vrindavan-vihara — descriptions of Krishna draw directly from Bilvamangala's verses. (Source: Chaitanya Charitamrita; Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition.)
